one_line_takeaway: MuCD uses Differential Privacy to give offline-RL policies the first practical, computable certificates of poisoning robustness - at both the per-state action level and the expected-cumulative-reward level - extending COPA to continuous/stochastic settings and tolerating ~7% poisoned data (vs COPA's 0.008%) for a <50% reward drop.
key_finding: MuCD certifies substantially stronger poisoning robustness than COPA while covering far more environments. On Freeway it certifies a <50% performance drop while tolerating 7.17% of the training data poisoned, versus only 0.008% for COPA; on Breakout the figures are 2.05% vs 0.0075%. Action-level certified radii are roughly 5x larger than COPA's. Higher training noise (sigma) trades clean reward for stronger certificates. Transition-level certificates tolerate ~10x larger r than trajectory-level (though because a trajectory is ~1000 transitions, the total transitions alterable is actually higher at trajectory level). DQN consistently certifies more robustly than C51, and RDP-derived bounds are consistently tighter than ADP.
core_contribution: The paper proposes MuCD (Multi-level Certified Defenses), the first practical certified-defense framework against data poisoning in general offline reinforcement learning. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a two-level poisoning threat taxonomy (trajectory-level, occurring during data collection and propagating downstream; and transition-level, occurring after collection with only the modified transitions affected); (2) two matching certification objectives it formalizes and proves - action-level robustness (guaranteeing the per-state chosen action is unchanged under up to r poisoning changes) and policy-level robustness (a computable lower bound Jr on the expected cumulative reward); and (3) a construction that attains these certificates by combining a Differential-Privacy-based randomized training process with new DP-'outcomes-guarantee' bounds. Crucially, unlike the prior COPA method it builds on (Wu et al., 2022), MuCD covers both discrete AND continuous action spaces and both deterministic AND stochastic environments, and it certifies the policy's expected cumulative reward rather than only specific repeatable trajectories.
threat_model: A data-poisoning adversary corrupting the externally-sourced offline RL training dataset. The paper defines two adversary variants, each budgeted by a size r of allowed changes (additions, deletions, or alterations). Trajectory-level (Def 3.1): the adversary has full knowledge of the MDP, can observe all historical transitions during the behavioral-policy data-collection process, and alters up to r whole trajectories; because changes occur during collection they propagate to subsequent transitions. Transition-level (Def 3.2): the adversary modifies up to r individual transitions after data is collected, with no downstream propagation, so it can precisely target key transitions. The defender wants worst-case (certified) guarantees that hold for ANY poisoned dataset within the budget r, with confidence at least 1-delta - either that the test-time action at a state is provably unchanged, or that expected cumulative reward stays above a provable lower bound.
limitations: The certificates are inherently probabilistic (hold with probability >= 1-delta, here delta=0.001), not absolute. Stronger robustness requires higher training noise, which measurably degrades clean performance (e.g., Freeway DQN clean reward drops from 20.1 at sigma=0 to 16.0 at sigma=3.0; Breakout DQN from 385.4 to 268.4). The framework uses two DIFFERENT DP mechanisms for the two threat levels rather than one unified algorithm, and the paper itself flags a unified transition-and-trajectory-level training algorithm and adaptive (non-uniform) noise injection as future work. The action-level certificate is defined for discrete action spaces. Empirical results are on relatively small benchmark games (Atari Freeway/Breakout, Mujoco Half Cheetah) rather than the healthcare/autonomous-driving domains cited as motivation.

datasets_used: Farama Gymnasium Atari 'Freeway' (discrete; 2M transitions, 976 trajectories, default length 2000)
Farama Gymnasium Atari 'Breakout' (discrete; 2M transitions, 3,648 trajectories, default length 600)
Mujoco 'Half Cheetah' (continuous action space; 2M transitions, 2,000 trajectories)
D4RL offline RL dataset (Fu et al. 2020)
Opacus DP training framework (Yousefpour et al. 2021)
quantitative_results: Freeway: MuCD tolerates 7.17% of training data poisoned for a <50% performance drop vs COPA's 0.008%
Breakout: MuCD tolerates 2.05% poisoned vs COPA's 0.0075%
Action-level certified radii ~5x larger than COPA
Freeway clean avg cumulative reward (DQN): 20.1 at sigma=0, 16.9/16.6/16.0 at sigma=1/2/3; C51: 21.3 at sigma=0
Freeway DQN action-level mean radii (transition-level): 128.1/145.5/160.0 at sigma=1/2/3, vs COPA 10.1 (trajectory-level)
Freeway DQN action-level mean radii (trajectory-level): 32.6/58.7/102.4 at sigma=1/2/3, vs COPA 10.1
Breakout clean avg cumulative reward (DQN): 385.4 at sigma=0, dropping to 268.4 at sigma=2.0; C51 389.3 at sigma=0
Breakout DQN action-level mean radii (transition-level): 3.4/7.9/17.7 at sigma=1/1.5/2, vs COPA 6.6 (trajectory-level)
Half Cheetah (IQL): benign avg cumulative reward 96.47; randomized-training reward 90.5/87.0/83.4 at sigma=1.0/2.0/3.0
Transition-level certification tolerates ~10x larger poisoning size r than trajectory-level for the same Jr
COPA's max certifiable cumulative reward limited to 5 (Freeway) and 2 (Breakout) due to tree-search length caps of 400 and 75
Configuration: p=50 policy instances, m=500 reward estimations (10 per instance), delta=0.001, batch size 32
baselines_compared: COPA (Wu et al., 2022), specifically the basic PARL variant with 50 partitions - the only prior practical RL poisoning certification; discrete/deterministic only
Internal ablations across noise multipliers sigma (1/1.5/2/3)
Internal comparison of RL algorithms DQN vs C51 (and IQL for continuous Half Cheetah)
Internal comparison of DP certification variants: Renyi-DP (RDP) vs Approximate-DP (ADP)
